content.full_text:
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 2, 1999

Cathereine L. DiCamillo
Office of the City Attorney
City of South Lake Tahoe
1052 Tata Lane
South Lake Tahoe, California 96150-6324

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-99-023

Dear Ms. DiCamillo:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Tom Davis, City Councilmember for the City of South Lake Tahoe, about the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

I. QUESTION

May Mr. Davis take a role in governmental decisions involving the Lake Tahoe Airport?

II. CONCLUSION

Since your question is general (i.e., it does not refer to a particular governmental decision), we can provide only general guidance. The following analysis provides an outline of the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions as they will probably apply to Mr. Davis in the context of decisions about the Airport and Tahoe Air.²

_________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Please note that we provide advice only about the Political Reform Act's conflict-of-interest provisions. Other ethical constraints which may apply to Mr. Davis, such as Government Code Sections 1090 et seq., and Government Code Section 1098, are outside the Commission's jurisdiction.

File No. I-99-023
Page 2

III. FACTS

Mr. Davis has accepted the position of Director of Community Relations with Tahoe Air.
Tahoe Air is a for-profit corporation that intends to provide commercial air service from the Lake
Tahoe Airport prior to June 30, 1999.

Tahoe Air rents airline offices at the Lake Tahoe Airport on a month-to-month basis,
paying rent of $1,042.38 per month. The renting of office space at the Airport is a function of the
Airport Commission, not the South Lake Tahoe City Council. Mr. Davis is the City Council
liaison to the Airport Commission, which is a non-voting position.

The City has issued a letter of commitment to Tahoe Air allocating it six of the available
ten slots for its airplanes. The air traffic control tower at the airport is partially funded by the
City of South Lake Tahoe's general fund. Issues affecting the funding of the air traffic control
tower, as well as other operations of the airport, come before the South Lake Tahoe City Council
for action from time to time.

IV. ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.

To say that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, is to conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step
analysis for deciding whether the Act's conflict-of-interest restrictions apply to a given
individual with regard to a given governmental decision. (Regulation 18700(b).) We provide the
following advice in the context of that eight-step analysis.

At the outset, it is important to note that the Act does not forbid any individual from
holding any position or employment, public or private. The Commission has no authority to
prevent or permit any person's employment. The Act's conflict-of-interest rules apply only to
determine whether a given public official has a conflict of interest in a given governmental
decision, given his or her economic interests and the degree to which the governmental decision
is likely to affect those economic interests.

File No. I-99-023
Page 3

A.     Mr. Davis' status as a public official.

     As a Member of the City Council, Mr. Davis will, of course, be considered a public
official subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest restrictions. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701.)

B.     The conflict-of-interest rules apply to making, participating in making, and
       influencing governmental decisions.

     The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply to Mr. Davis when he makes, participates
in making, or in any way attempts to use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)
As explained in part IV.C, below, Mr. Davis has certain economic interests which are potentially
implicated by governmental decisions about the Airport in general, and Tahoe Air in particular.
Mr. Davis must consider if his intended actions with regard to any such matters constitute
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision—if so, a conflict of
interest is possible.

     The Commission's regulations describe in some detail what constitutes making,
participating in making, and influencing a governmental decision. (Regulations 18702.1,
18702.2, and 18702.3, respectively.) There are also important exceptions to these rules.
(Regulation 18702.4.) Since you have not referred to a particular governmental decision in your
advice request, we can only provide you with the following outline of these provisions.

     A public official, such as Mr. Davis, "makes a governmental decision," when the official,
acting within the authority of his or her office or position, does any of the following:

     •     votes on a matter,
     •     appoints a person,
     •     obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action,
     •     enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency,
     •     or determines not to do any of these things, unless such determination is made because of
           his or her financial interest.3
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(1)-(4).)

     A public official "participates in making a governmental decision," when, acting within
the authority of his or her position, the official does any of the following:

_______________________________
3     When the determination not to act occurs because of the official's financial interest, the official's
determination must be accompanied by disclosure of the financial interest, made part of the agency's official record,
or made in writing to the official's supervisor as provided in 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18730(b)(10), to the
appointing power, or to any other person specified in a Conflict of Interest Code adopted pursuant to Government
Code Section 87300.

File No. I-99-023
Page 4

• negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental entity or private person regarding a governmental decision,
• advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker either directly or without significant intervening substantive review, by conducting research or making any investigation which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which is to influence a governmental decision, or by preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion, orally, or in writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which is to influence a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18702.2(a), (b).)

However, a public official neither makes nor participate in making a governmental decision by doing any of the following:

• Taking actions which are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical;
• Making appearances as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the official's personal interests;
• Taking actions relating to his or her compensation or the terms or conditions of his or her employment or contract. In the case of public officials who are 'consultants,' as defined above, this includes actions by consultants relating to the terms or conditions of the contract pursuant to which they provide services to the agency, so long as they are acting in their private capacity.
(Regulation 18702.4(a).)

There are two rules concerning whether a public official uses or attempts to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision. The first rule applies when the relevant governmental decision is within or before the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) In that case, "... the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer." (Ibid.)

However, an official is not attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if he or she does any of the following:

• Appears in the same manner as any other member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function solely to represent himself or herself on a matter which is related to his or her personal interests. An official's "personal interests" include, but are not limited to, an interest in real property which is wholly

File No. I-99-023
Page 5

owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family; a business entity
wholly owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family; or, a business
entity over which the official exercises sole direction and control, or over which the
official and his or her spouse jointly exercise sole direction and control.
●
Communicates with the general public or the press;
●
Negotiates his or her compensation or the terms and conditions of his or her employment
or contract.
●
Prepares drawings or submissions of an architectural, engineering or similar nature to be
used by a client in connection with a proceeding before any agency. However, this
provision applies only if the official has no other direct oral or written contact with the
agency with regard to the client's proceeding before the agency except for necessary
contact with agency staff concerning the processing or evaluation of the drawings or
submissions prepared by the official.
●
Appears before a design or architectural review committee or similar body of which he or
she is a member to present drawings or submissions of an architectural, engineering or
similar nature which the official has prepared for a client if the following three criteria are
met: (A) The review committee's sole function is to review architectural or engineering
plans or designs and to make recommendations in that instance concerning those plans or
designs to a planning commission or other agency; (B) The ordinance or other provision
of law requires that the review committee include architects, engineers or persons in
related professions, and the official was appointed to the body to fulfill this requirement;
and (C) The official is a sole practitioner.
(Regulation 18702.4(b).)

The second rule applies when the relevant governmental decision is within or before an
agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(b).) In that case, "... the
official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the
purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the
representative of, his or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency.
Such actions include, but are not limited to the use of official stationery." (Ibid.)

C.

Identifying economic interests.

1. Introduction.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. The "economic interests" from which conflicts of interest may arise are defined in
Regulations 18703-18703.5. There are six kinds of such economic interests:

File No. I-99-023
Page 6

•
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment⁴ of $1,000 or more (Regulation 18703.1(a));
•
A public official has as an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management
(Regulation 18703.1(b));
•
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct
or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Regulation 18703.2);
•
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Regulation 18703.3);
•
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $290 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Regulation 18703.4);
•
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets,
or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family—this is known as the
"personal financial effects" rule (Regulation 18703.5).

2. Mr. Davis' economic interests.

(a) Mr. Davis has an economic interest in Tahoe Air.

Your advice request indicates that Mr. Davis has accepted an offer of employment from
Tahoe Air. An accepted offer of employment, whatever its "effective date," is a promise by the
offeror to provide income in return for services. Mr. Davis thus has a current economic interest
in Tahoe Air because it is a source of promised income. (Section 87103(c); Regulation
18703.3(a).)

Mr. Davis also has, or will have, an economic interest in Tahoe Air by virtue of holding a
position as an employee of that business entity. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

(b) Mr. Davis has an economic interest in his personal finances, as well as those of his
immediate family.

Under what is commonly called the "personal financial effect" rule, Mr. Davis has an
economic interest in his personal finances (i.e., his personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities), and those of his immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

_________________________

⁴ An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent
child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the
official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent
interest or greater. (Section 87103.)

File No. I-99-023
Page 7

D.        Determining whether Mr. Davis' economic interests are directly or indirectly
         involved in a governmental decision.

         The fourth step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest is to determine whether each
of the public official's economic interests is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision at-issue. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) (It must be remembered that conflict-of-interest
issues under the Act arise only in the context of particular governmental decisions.) This step is
important because it helps determine (in the fifth step) which test for materiality to use in
deciding whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on the economic interests.

         1. Tahoe Air.

         Now that it has been determined that Mr. Davis has an economic interest in Tahoe Air, a
business entity, the next step in the analysis is determining the degree to which Tahoe Air is
involved in the government decision in question. A business entity is directly involved in a
decision when it:

         •      Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
                claim, appeal, or similar request (Regulation 18704.1(a)(1));
         •      Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
                official or the official's agency. A source of income is the subject of a proceeding if a
                decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license,
                permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.
                (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).)

         Thus, for any particular governmental decision, if either of the preceding criteria are true
as to Tahoe Air, Tahoe Air will be deemed directly involved in the decision. If, on the other
hand, none of the criteria are true, Tahoe Air will be deemed indirectly involved in the
governmental decision for purposes of choosing a materiality standard. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)

         2. Personal financial effect.

         Mr. Davis' personal finances and those of his immediate family are deemed to be directly
involved in any governmental decision which has an effect on them. (Regulation 18704.5.)

File No. I-99-023
Page 8

E.        Deciding which materiality standards to use to decide if there will be a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect.

1. Tahoe Air.

(a) Materiality standards to use when Tahoe Air is directly involved in the governmental
decision-in-question.

When Tahoe Air is directly involved in the governmental decision in question (see part
IV.D.1, above), any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Tahoe Air is deemed material.
(Regulation 18705.1(a).) That is, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision in
question will have any financial effect—even a penny's worth—on Tahoe Air, Mr. Davis will
have a conflict of interest in that decision, unless the public generally exception applies.
(Regulation 18706.)

(b) Materiality standards to use when Tahoe Air is indirectly involved in the
governmental decision in question.

Regulation 18705.1(b) prescribes materiality standards to be used when the business
entity in question is indirectly involved in a governmental decision. Subdivisions (b)(1)-(b)(7)
prescribe alternative standards; which one applies depends primarily on the size of the business
entity.

When Tahoe Air is indirectly involved in a City Council decision, whether Mr. Davis has
a conflict of interest depends on the following question: is it reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will result in one or more of the applicable materiality standards being true as to Tahoe
Air? (Regulation 18706.) If the answer is "yes," he will have a conflict of interest unless the
public generally exception applies. If the answer is "no," Mr. Davis will not have a conflict of
interest.

2. Personal financial effect.

The financial effect of a governmental decision on Mr. Davis' personal finances, and
those of his immediate family, is deemed material if it is at least $250 in any twelve-month
period. (Regulation 18705.5.) That is, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will increase or decrease the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of
Mr. Davis or his immediate family by $250 in any twelve-month period, he has a conflict of
interest, unless the public generally exception applies. (Regulation 18706.)

File No. 1-99-023
Page 9

F. The "public generally exception."

Even if a public official otherwise has a conflict of interest, he or she may still be able to
take a role in the government decision in question. If the reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect of a governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is
indistinguishable "from its effect on the public generally," then the public official does not have
a conflict. (Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) This rule is referred to as the "public
generally exception." Specifically, the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a
public official's financial interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally if it
is also reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect a "significant segment" of the public
in substantially the same manner "if it will affect the public official's economic interest.
(Regulation 18707(b)(1),(2).) This exception exists because a public official is less likely to be
biased by a financial impact on his or her economic interests when a significant part of the
community is substantially likely to feel essentially the same impact from the governmental
decision.

It is impossible to analyze the potential applicability of the public generally exception
without a particular factual situation in mind. However, we note that it seems unlikely that the
exception will apply to conflicts arising from either of Mr. Davis' economic interests. As to
Tahoe Air, it seems highly unlikely that a significant segment of the jurisdiction will feel a
financial effect which is substantially similar to the effect on an airline. Similarly, as to a
material personal financial effect on Mr. Davis resulting from a decision about the Airport or
Tahoe Air, it seems unlikely that a significant segment of the population will feel a financial
effect substantially similar to the one felt by an employee of the airline. However, as stated
above, each particular factual situation must be judged on its own merits.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John Vergelli
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

decision), we can provide only general guidance. The following analysis provides an outline of
the Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions as they will probably apply to Mr. Davis in the context
of decisions about the Airport and Tahoe Air.

18995, of the California Code ofRegulations.

Other ethical constraints which may apply to Mr. Davis, such as Government Code Sections 1090 et seq., and
Government Code Section 1098, are outside the Commission’s jurisdiction.
ifi. FACTS
Mr. Davis has accepted the position ofDirector of Community Relations with Tahoe Air.
Tahoe Air is a for-profit corporation that intends to provide commercial air service from the Lake
Tahoe Airport prior to June 30, 1999.
Tahoe Air rents airline offices at the Lake Tahoe Airport on a month-to-month basis,
paying rent of $1,042.38 per month. The renting of office space at the Airport is a function ofthe
Airport Commission, not the South Lake Tahoe City Council. Mr. Davis is the City Council
liaison to the Airport Commission, which is a non-voting position.
The City has issued a letter of commitment to Tahoe Air allocating it six ofthe available
ten slots for its airplanes.
The air traffic control tower at the airport is partially funded by the
City of South Lake Tahoe’s general fund. Issues affecting the funding ofthe air traffic control
tower, as well as other operations ofthe airport, come before the South Lake Tahoe City Council
for action from time to time.
IV. ANALYSIS
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests ofpersons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.
To say that a public official has a “financial interest” in a governmental decision, within
the meaning ofthe Act, is to conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official’s economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step

official subject to the Act’s conflict-of-interest restrictions. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701.)

When the determination not to act occurs because ofthe official’s financial interest, the official’s

•

governmental decision.
(Regulation 18702.2(a), (b).)

•

solely to the official’s personal interests;
•

employment or contract. In the case ofpublic officials who are ‘consultants,’ as defined

contract pursuant to which they provide services to the agency, so long as they are acting

(Regulation 18702.4(a).)
There are two rules concerning whether a public official uses or attempts to use his or her

18702.3(a).) In that case,

of the agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by

However, an official is

governmental decision ifhe or she does any ofthe following:
•

in the course ofits prescribed governmental function solely to represent himself or herself

interests” include, but are not limited to, an interest in real property which is wholly
owned by the official or members ofhis or her immediate family; a business entity
wholly owned by the official or members ofhis or her immediate family; or, a business
entity over which the official exercises sole direction and control, or over which the
official and his or her spouse jointly exercise sole direction and control.
•
Communicates with the general public or the press;
•
Negotiates his or her compensation or the terms and conditions ofhis or her employment
or contract.
•
Prepares drawings or submissions of an architectural, engineering or similar nature to be
used by a client in connection with a proceeding before any agency. However, this
provision applies only ifthe official has no other direct oral or written contact with the
agency with regard to the client’s proceeding before the agency except for necessary
contact with agency staff concerning the processing or evaluation ofthe drawings or
submissions prepared by the official.
•
Appears before a design or architectural review committee or similar body ofwhich he or
she is a member to present drawings or submissions of an architectural, engineering or
similar nature which the official has prepared for a client ifthe following three criteria are
met: (A) The review committee’s sole function is to review architectural or engineering
plans or designs and to make recommendations in that instance concerning those plans or
designs to a planning commission or other agency; (B) The ordinance or other provision
of law requires that the review committee include architects, engineers or persons in
related professions, and the officialwas appointed to the body to fulfill this requirement;
and (C) The official is a sole practitioner.
(Regulation 18702.4(b).)
The second rule applies when the relevant governmental decision is within or before an
agency other than the public official’s own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control ofthe public official’s agency. (Regulation 18702.3(b).) In that case, “... the
official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the
purpose ofinfluencing the decision, the official acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the
representative of, his or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency.
Such actions include, but are not limited to the use of official stationery.” (Ibid.)
C.
Identifying economic interests.
1. Introduction.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. The “economic interests” from which conflicts of interest may arise are defined in
Regulations 18703-18703.5.
There are six kinds of such economic interests:
•
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment
4 of $1,000 or more (Regulation 18703.1(a));
•
A public official has as an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds anyposition ofmanagement
(Regulation 18703.1(b));
•
A public official has an economic interest in realproperty in which he or she has a direct
or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Regulation 18703.2);
•
A public official has an economic interest in any source ofincome, including promised
income, which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Regulation 18703.3);
•
A public official has an economic interest in any source ofgifts to him or her ifthe gifts
aggregate to $290 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Regulation 18703.4);
•
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets,
or liabilities, as well as those ofhis or her immediate family—this is lcnown as the
“personal financial effects” rule (Regulation 18703.5).
2. Mr. Davis’ economic interests.
(a) Mr. Davis has an economic interest in Tahoe Air.
Your advice request indicates that Mr. Davis has accepted an offer of employment from
Tahoe Air. An accepted offer of employment, whatever its “effective date,” is a promise by the
offeror to provide income in return for services. Mr. Davis thus has a current economic interest
in Tahoe Air because it is a source ofpromised income. (Section 87103(c); Regulation
18703.3(a).)
Mr. Davis also has, or will have, an economic interest in Tahoe Air by virtue ofholding a
position as an employee ofthat business entity. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)
(b) Mr. Davis has an economic interest in his personalfinances, as well as those ofhis
immediatefamily.
Under what is commonly called the “personal financial effect” rule, Mr. Davis has an
economic interest in his personal finances (i.e., his personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities), and those ofhis immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent
child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the
official, the official’s agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent
interest or greater. (Section 87103.)
D.
Determining whether Mr. Davis’ economic interests are directly or indirectly
involved in a governmental decision.
The fourth step in analyzing a potential conflict ofinterest is to determine whether each
ofthe public official’s economic interests is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision at-issue. (Regulation 1 8700(b)(4).) (It must be remembered that conflict-of-interest
issues under the Act arise only in the context ofparticular governmental decisions.) This step is
important because it helps determine (in the fifth step) which test for materiality to use in
deciding whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on the economic interests.
1. Tahoe Air.
Now that it has been determined that Mr. Davis has an economic interest in Tahoe Air, a
business entity, the next step in the analysis is determining the degree to which Tahoe Air is
involved in the government decision in question. A business entity is directly involved in a
decision when it:
•
Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request (Regulation 18704. 1(a)(1));
•
Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency. A source ofincome is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.
(Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).)
Thus, for any particular governmental decision, if either ofthe preceding criteria are true
as to Tahoe Air, Tahoe Air will be deemed directly involved in the decision. If, on the other
hand, none ofthe criteria are true, Tahoe Air will be deemed indirectly involved in the
governmental decision for purposes of choosing a materiality standard. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)
2. Personal financial effect.
Mr. Davis’ personal finances and those ofhis immediate family are deemed to be directly
involved in any governmental decision which has an effect on them. (Regulation 18704.5.)
E.
Deciding which materiality standards to use to decide if there will be a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect.
1. Tahoe Air.
(a) Materiality standards to use when Tahoe Air is directly involved in the governmental
decision-in-question.
When Tahoe Air is directly involved in the governmental decision in question (see part
IV.D. 1, above), any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Tahoe Air is deemed material.
(Regulation 18705.1(a).) That is, ifit is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision in
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: numbered, ocr; CONCLUSION appears before QUESTION

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: will have any financial effect—even a penny’s worth—on Tahoe Air, Mr. Davis will
have a conflict of interest in that decision, unless the public generally exception applies.
(Regulation 18706.)
(b) Materiality standards to use when Tahoe Air is indirectly involved in the
governmental decision in question.
Regulation 18705.1(b) prescribes materiality standards to be used when the business
entity in question is indirectly involved in a governmental decision. Subdivisions (b)(l)-(b)(7)
prescribe alternative standards; which one applies depends primarily on the size ofthe business
entity.
When Tahoe Air is indirectly involved in a City Council decision, whether Mr. Davis has
a conflict of interest depends on the following question: is it reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will result in one or more ofthe applicable materiality standards being true as to Tahoe
Air? (Regulation 18706.) Ifthe answer is “yes,” he will have a conflict ofinterest unless the
public generally exception applies. Ifthe answer is “no,” Mr. Davis will not have a conflict of
interest.
2. Personal financial effect.
The financial effect of a governmental decision on Mr. Davis’ personal finances, and
those ofhis immediate family, is deemed material ifit is at least $250 in any twelve-month
period. (Regulation 18705.5.) That is, ifit is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will increase or decrease the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of
Mr. Davis or his immediate family by $250 in any twelve-month period, he has a conflict of
interest, unless the public generally exception applies. (Regulation 18706.)
F. The “public generally exception.”
Even if a public official otherwise has a conflict ofinterest, he or she may still be able to
take a role in the government decision in question. Ifthe reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect of a governmental decision on the public official’s economic interest is
indistinguishable “from its effect on the public generally,” then the public official does not have
a conflict. (Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) This rule is referred to as the “public
generally exception.” Specifically, the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a
public official’s financial interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally if it
is also reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect a “significant segment” ofthe public
“in substantially the same manner” it will affect the public official’s economic interest.
(Regulation 1 8707(b)(1),(2).) This exception exists because a public official is less likely to be
biased by a financial impact on his or her economic interests when a significant part ofthe
community is substantially likely to feel essentially the same impact from the governmental
decision.
It is impossible to analyze the potential applicability ofthe public generally exception
without a particular factual situation in mind. However, we note that it seems unlilcely that the
exception will apply to conflicts arising from either ofMr. Davis’ economic interests. As to
Tahoe Air, it seems highly unlilcely that a significant segment ofthe jurisdiction will feel a
financial effect which is substantially similar to the effect on an airline. Similarly, as to a
material personal financial effect on Mr. Davis resulting from a decision about the Airport or
Tahoe Air, it seems unlikely that a significant segment of the population will feel a financial
effect substantially similar to the one felt by an employee ofthe airline. However, as stated
above, each particular factual situation must be judged on its own merits.
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

CONCLUSION: Since your question is general (i.e., it does not refer to a particular governmental
decision), we can provide only general guidance. The following analysis provides an outline of
the Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions as they will probably apply to Mr. Davis in the context
of decisions about the Airport and Tahoe Air.
2
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code ofRegulations.
2 Please note that we provide advice only about the Political Reform Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions.
Other ethical constraints which may apply to Mr. Davis, such as Government Code Sections 1090 et seq., and
Government Code Section 1098, are outside the Commission’s jurisdiction.
ifi. FACTS
Mr. Davis has accepted the position ofDirector of Community Relations with Tahoe Air.
Tahoe Air is a for-profit corporation that intends to provide commercial air service from the Lake
Tahoe Airport prior to June 30, 1999.
Tahoe Air rents airline offices at the Lake Tahoe Airport on a month-to-month basis,
paying rent of $1,042.38 per month. The renting of office space at the Airport is a function ofthe
Airport Commission, not the South Lake Tahoe City Council. Mr. Davis is the City Council
liaison to the Airport Commission, which is a non-voting position.
The City has issued a letter of commitment to Tahoe Air allocating it six ofthe available
ten slots for its airplanes.
The air traffic control tower at the airport is partially funded by the
City of South Lake Tahoe’s general fund. Issues affecting the funding ofthe air traffic control
tower, as well as other operations ofthe airport, come before the South Lake Tahoe City Council
for action from time to time.
IV. ANALYSIS
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests ofpersons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.
To say that a public official has a “financial interest” in a governmental decision, within
the meaning ofthe Act, is to conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official’s economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step

QUESTION May Mr. Davis take a role in governmental decisions involving the Lake Tahoe Airport? II.
